title: Managing Temperature Excursions
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical, risk-based guide to managing a temperature excursion under EU GDP and MHRA: detection, investigation, disposition and CAPA done right.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. Close, shallow depth-of-field shot of a refrigerated cold-chain shipping container interior: rows of vialed medicines nestled in temperature-controlled packaging beside a sleek digital data logger displaying a clean temperature graph, condensation faintly misting the chilled surfaces. Soft natural daylight rakes across brushed-steel and frosted-glass surfaces, catching gentle highlights on the vials while the background dissolves into a calm navy bokeh. Premium, sterile, reassuring and precise; 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no watermarks.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a temperature alarm and a temperature excursion?
faqs.a: An alarm is a monitoring trigger indicating that a sensor has read outside a set threshold, which may be caused by a door opening, sensor placement or a brief air-space fluctuation. An excursion is a confirmed deviation of the product itself outside its labelled storage conditions. Every alarm should be reviewed, but only verified deviations affecting product are treated as excursions requiring formal assessment and disposition.

faqs.q: Can a product be released after a temperature excursion?
faqs.a: Yes, provided the cumulative exposure falls within validated stability limits or manufacturer-approved permitted-excursion data, and the decision is made by a competent Responsible Person or Qualified Person. The assessment must compare verified duration and magnitude against the approved stability budget, accounting for any prior excursions in the product's history. The rationale and supporting data must be fully documented under ALCOA+ principles so the decision is reconstructable.

faqs.q: Who is responsible for temperature excursion decisions under UK GDP?
faqs.a: For distributed medicinal products, the Responsible Person (RP) is accountable for GDP compliance, quarantine and disposition decisions. Where batch certification or recall judgement is engaged, the Qualified Person (QP) becomes involved. The key principle is that an independent, competent and named decision-maker, insulated from commercial pressure, holds the authority to reject product where the data does not support release.
